---
title: "Superfund Maps"
author: "Fintan Mooney"
date: "2024-12-05"
output:
  html_document: default
  word_document: default
  pdf_document: default
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(classInt) # For classification intervals
library(gridExtra)


```


```{r,load, echo = FALSE}
# Define the file path to your shapefile
shapefile_path <- "C:/Users/moone/OneDrive - Columbia University Irving Medical Center/MiscResearch/Superfund/Data/EJScreen_SF_LI_Shapefile/EJScreen_SF_LI_Shapefile.shp"

# Load the shapefile into R as an sf object
data <- st_read(shapefile_path)

# Display the first few rows to understand the structure
print(head(data))

colnames(data)
```

## Subset columns

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r dem, echo=FALSE}

# Subset the columns
dem <- data %>%
  select(White_PCT, Black_PCT, HISPLA_PCT, Asian_PCT, LOWINCPCT, LINGISOPCT, LESSHSPCT)

# Rename columns with spaces
dem <- dem %>%
  rename(
    `White Percent` = White_PCT,
    `Black Percent` = Black_PCT,
    `Hispanic Percent` = HISPLA_PCT,
    `Asian Percent` = Asian_PCT,
    `Low Income Percent` = LOWINCPCT,
    `Limited English Percent` = LINGISOPCT,
    `Less than High School Percent` = LESSHSPCT
  )

# View the renamed dataframe
print(head(dem))
```
```{r, counties, echo = FALSE}

# Dissolve the census tract data by COUNTYFP to create county boundaries
counties <- data %>%
  group_by(COUNTYFP) %>%          # Group by the COUNTYFP column
  summarize(geometry = st_union(geometry)) %>%  # Dissolve geometries within each county
  ungroup()                   

# Optional: Filter for Nassau (COUNTYFP = '059') and Suffolk (COUNTYFP = '103')
counties <- counties %>%
  filter(COUNTYFP %in% c("059", "103"))

# View the counties object
print(counties)
```


```{r, create maps, echo=FALSE}
# List of variables to map
variables <- c(
  "White Percent",
  "Black Percent",
  "Hispanic Percent",
  "Asian Percent",
  "Low Income Percent",
  "Limited English Percent",
  "Less than High School Percent"
)

# Create maps for each variable using ggplot
plots <- lapply(variables, function(var) {
  
  # Scale column values by 100
  dem <- dem %>%
    mutate(!!sym(var) := !!sym(var) * 100)
  
  # Create equal interval classification and generate custom labels
  breaks <- classIntervals(pull(dem, !!sym(var)), n = 5, style = "equal")$brks
  labels <- paste0("≤ ", round(breaks[-1], 1), "%")  # Add a percent sign to the labels
  
  dem <- dem %>%
    mutate(class = cut(!!sym(var), 
                       breaks = breaks, 
                       include.lowest = TRUE,
                       labels = labels))  # Assign percent labels
  
  # Plot the map with a discrete color palette
  ggplot() +
    geom_sf(data = dem, aes(fill = class), lwd = 0.1) +  # Remove tract borders
    geom_sf(data = counties, fill = NA, color = "red", lwd = 0.5) +  # Add county borders
    scale_fill_brewer(
      palette = "YlGnBu", 
      labels = labels,   # Use custom percent labels
      guide = guide_legend(
        title = "Equal Interval Breaks",     # Add legend title
        title.position = "top",              # Place title above the legend symbols
        title.hjust = 0.5,                   # Center the title
        title.theme = element_text(size = 8), # Style the legend title (not bolded)
        label.position = "bottom",           # Place labels below symbols
        nrow = 1                             # Make the legend single-row
      )
    ) +
    labs(
      title = paste(var, "in Long Island Census Tracts (2023)")
    ) +
    theme_void() +  # Remove background and coordinates
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 9, face = "bold", margin = margin(b = 5)), # Move title down
      legend.position = "top",              # Place legend on top
      legend.direction = "horizontal",      # Horizontal layout
      legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
      legend.key.width = unit(2, "cm"),     # Adjust legend width
      legend.key.height = unit(0.5, "cm"),  # Adjust legend height
      legend.margin = margin(t = 10, r = 0, b = 0, l = 0, unit = "pt"), # Add space between legend and title
      plot.margin = margin(t = 20, r = 10, b = 10, l = 10, unit = "pt") # Add space above and around the plot
    )
})

# Print each plot separately
for (plot in plots) {
  print(plot)
}

```

```{r, env df, echo = FALSE}
# Subset the columns
env <- data %>%
  select(PRE1960PCT, PM25,OZONE, RSEI_AIR, PTRAF,UST)

# Rename columns with spaces
env <- env %>%
  rename(
    `Lead Indicator` = PRE1960PCT,
    `Annual Average PM2.5 Concentrations (ug/m3)` = PM25,
    `Average Highest Ozone Levels (ppm)` = OZONE,
    `Toxic Releases to Air (RESI Score)` = RSEI_AIR,
    `Traffic Proximity and Volume` = PTRAF,
    `Proximity to Underground Storage Tanks` = UST
  )

# View the renamed dataframe
print(head(dem))


```

```{r, lead, echo=FALSE}
# List of variables to map
variables <- c(
  "Lead Indicator"
)

# Create maps for each variable using ggplot
plots <- lapply(variables, function(var) {
  
  # Scale column values by 100
  env <- env %>%
    mutate(!!sym(var) := !!sym(var) * 100)
  
  # Create equal interval classification and generate custom labels
  breaks <- classIntervals(pull(env, !!sym(var)), n = 5, style = "equal")$brks
  labels <- paste0("≤ ", round(breaks[-1], 1), "%")  # Add a percent sign to the labels
  
  env <- env %>%
    mutate(class = cut(!!sym(var), 
                       breaks = breaks, 
                       include.lowest = TRUE,
                       labels = labels))  # Assign percent labels
  
  # Plot the map with a discrete color palette
  ggplot() +
    geom_sf(data = env, aes(fill = class), lwd = 0.1) +  # Remove tract borders
    geom_sf(data = counties, fill = NA, color = "red", lwd = 0.5) +  # Add county borders
    scale_fill_brewer(
      palette = "YlGnBu", 
      labels = labels,   # Use custom percent labels
      name = "Percent of Housing Units Built Pre-1960 (Equal Interval Breaks)",  # Legend title
      guide = guide_legend(
        title.position = "top",     # Place title above the legend symbols
        title.hjust = 0.5,          # Center the title
        title.theme = element_text(size = 8), # Style the legend title
        label.position = "bottom",  # Place labels below symbols
        nrow = 1                    # Make the legend single-row
      )
    ) +
    labs(
      title = paste(var, "for Long Island Census Tracts (2023)")
    ) +
    theme_void() +  # Remove background and coordinates
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 9, face = "bold", margin = margin(b = 10)), # Move title down
      legend.position = "top",              # Place legend on top
      legend.direction = "horizontal",      # Horizontal layout
      legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
      legend.key.width = unit(2, "cm"),     # Adjust legend width
      legend.key.height = unit(0.5, "cm"),  # Adjust legend height
      legend.margin = margin(t = 10, r = 0, b = 0, l = 0, unit = "pt"), # Add space between legend and title
      plot.margin = margin(t = 20, r = 10, b = 10, l = 10, unit = "pt") # Add space above and around the plot
    )
})

# Print each plot separately
for (plot in plots) {
  print(plot)
}

```

```{r, env maps, echo=FALSE}

# List of variables to map
variables <- c(
  "Annual Average PM2.5 Concentrations (ug/m3)",
  "Average Highest Ozone Levels (ppm)",
  "Toxic Releases to Air (RESI Score)",
  "Traffic Proximity and Volume",
  "Proximity to Underground Storage Tanks"
)

# Create maps for each variable using ggplot
plots <- lapply(variables, function(var) {
  
  # Filter out NA values (if any) for robustness
  env_var <- env %>%
    filter(!is.na(!!sym(var)))
  
  # Pull the data for the variable
  var_data <- pull(env_var, !!sym(var))
  
  # Use quantile classification and ensure unique breaks
  breaks <- classIntervals(var_data, n = 5, style = "quantile")$brks
  
  # Adjust breaks to fully encompass the data range
  breaks[1] <- min(var_data) - 0.001  # Ensure lowest value is included
  breaks[length(breaks)] <- max(var_data) + 0.001  # Ensure highest value is included
  
  # Avoid duplicate breaks by ensuring unique and sorted values
  breaks <- sort(unique(round(breaks, digits = 6)))  # Adjust precision to avoid floating-point issues
  
  # Customize labels: round only Traffic Proximity and Volume, keep others as decimals
  if (var == "Traffic Proximity and Volume") {
    labels <- paste0("≤ ", round(breaks[-1], 0))  # Round Traffic to integers
  } else {
    labels <- paste0("≤ ", round(breaks[-1], 1))  # Keep one decimal for other variables
  }
  
  # Create classification using the corrected breaks
  env_var <- env_var %>%
    mutate(class = cut(!!sym(var), 
                       breaks = breaks, 
                       include.lowest = TRUE,  # Include lowest values
                       right = TRUE,           # Include upper bounds
                       labels = labels))
  
  # Check for any remaining NA in 'class' after cut()
  if (any(is.na(env_var$class))) {
    warning(paste("Warning: NA values found in 'class' for variable", var))
    print(env_var %>% filter(is.na(class)))  # Print problematic rows
    stop("Check breaks or data values to ensure alignment.")
  }
  
  # Plot the map with a discrete color palette
  ggplot() +
    geom_sf(data = env_var, aes(fill = class), lwd = 0.1) +  # Thin tract borders
    geom_sf(data = counties, fill = NA, color = "red", lwd = 0.5) +  # Add county borders
    scale_fill_brewer(
      palette = "YlGnBu", 
      labels = labels,   # Use custom labels
      guide = guide_legend(
        title = "Quantile Breaks",           # Add legend title
        title.position = "top",              # Place title above the legend
        title.hjust = 0.5,                   # Center the legend title
        title.theme = element_text(size = 8), # Style the legend title
        label.position = "bottom",           # Place labels below symbols
        nrow = 1                             # Single-row layout
      )
    ) +
    labs(
      title = paste(var, "in Long Island Census Tracts (2023)")
    ) +
    theme_void() +  # Remove background and coordinates
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 9, face = "bold", margin = margin(b = 5)), # Move title down
      legend.position = "top",              # Place legend on top
      legend.direction = "horizontal",      # Horizontal layout
      legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
      legend.key.width = unit(2, "cm"),     # Adjust legend width
      legend.key.height = unit(0.5, "cm"),  # Adjust legend height
      legend.margin = margin(t = 10, r = 0, b = 0, l = 0, unit = "pt"), # Add space between legend and title
      plot.margin = margin(t = 20, r = 10, b = 10, l = 10, unit = "pt") # Add space above and around the plot
    )
})

# Print each plot separately
for (plot in plots) {
  print(plot)
}


```
